longTailKeywords: how to treat keloid at home

h1Title: Keloid Treatment: Symptoms, Causes, and Removal Options
heroOverview: Keloid scars are benign but often symptomatic proliferative fibrotic growths that extend beyond the original wound boundary — distinguishing them from hypertrophic scars, which stay within the wound margins. They arise from dysregulated fibroblast activity and excess collagen deposition following cutaneous injury, inflammation, or even spontaneous skin irritation. Keloids affect 5–16% of the global population, with rates 15× higher in people with darker skin tones; earlobes, shoulders, chest, and upper back are most commonly involved. Pruritus, pain, and cosmetic distress significantly impact quality of life. Treatment requires a multimodal approach: surgery alone carries 45–100% recurrence rates, but surgery combined with adjuvant radiotherapy or cryotherapy reduces recurrence below 10%.

keyStats.prevalence: 5–16% global prevalence; affects Afro-Caribbean, Hispanic, and Asian populations at 5–15× the rate of people with lighter skin (Marneros & Krieg, JDDG 2004)
keyStats.demographics: Equal incidence in men and women; peak onset in second and third decades (ages 10–30); positive family history present in 10–15% of keloid patients
keyStats.avgAge: Peak onset age 10–30 years; spontaneous involution is rare; the earlobe, sternum, shoulder, and jawline are the most affected sites
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100 million new scars annually in developed countries; 55–100 million of these patients have post-surgical or post-traumatic scars; keloids represent the most treatment-resistant end of the pathological scar spectrum

definition: Keloid scar (ICD-10: L91.0) is an abnormal fibro-proliferative dermal growth that extends beyond the boundaries of the original wound and does not regress spontaneously — distinguishing it from hypertrophic scars, which remain within wound margins and often regress over 12–18 months. Keloids are composed of disorganized, thickened collagen bundles (predominantly type I collagen with excess type III), elevated transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β) activity, reduced apoptosis of fibroblasts, and increased angiogenesis. The result is a shiny, firm, often raised lesion that grows progressively over months to years. Keloids form at sites of prior trauma — surgery, burns, ear piercing, acne, vaccination, insect bites, or even without identified skin injury in predisposed individuals. The condition has a strong genetic component: polygenic inheritance with loci on chromosomes 2q23 and 7p11 associated with susceptibility. Management requires a plastic surgeon or dermatologist with experience in pathological scar treatment; recurrence is the dominant challenge and long-term follow-up is essential.

typesClassification.description: Small keloids at low-risk sites such as earlobes, face, and extremities. Intralesional corticosteroid injection alone or combined with cryotherapy produces the best outcomes with lowest recurrence risk.
Larger lesions on the chest, shoulders, or back. Require multimodal treatment; surgical excision combined with adjuvant intralesional steroids or radiotherapy is the standard approach.

Occupationally or cosmetically debilitating; often from burn injuries, post-surgical wounds, or inflammatory acne. Require staged surgical excision with radiation or pressure therapy and long-term monitoring.

Forms in the absence of identifiable skin trauma; most common on the midsternal area and upper back. Suggests a particularly strong genetic predisposition; treatment results are generally less satisfactory.
Keloids with mast-cell-driven severe pruritus or neuropathic pain require pharmacological management (antihistamines, pregabalin) in addition to structural scar treatment.

primarySymptoms: A raised, firm, shiny fibrous scar that extends beyond the original wound edge and continues to grow over weeks to months — the defining clinical feature of a keloid.
Intense pruritus (itching) at and around the keloid, driven by mast-cell degranulation and histamine release within the collagen matrix; reported in 86% of patients.
Burning or neuropathic pain within the keloid, particularly in larger lesions on the chest and shoulder, affecting daily activities and sleep.
Progressive cosmetic disfigurement as the lesion expands — often to a size disproportionate to the original injury — causing significant psychological distress and social withdrawal.
Restriction of movement when keloids involve mobile areas such as the anterior chest, axilla, neck, or antecubital fossa, from tethering of underlying soft tissue.
Skin surface discoloration — typically hyperpigmented and darker than the surrounding skin in all skin types — with a smooth or nodular surface texture.
Tenderness on palpation, particularly in active growing keloids, distinguishing them from stable mature scars.
Recurrent breakdown and ulceration in large keloids subjected to chronic friction from clothing, particularly over the shoulders and chest.
Psychological sequelae including depression, social anxiety, and body dysmorphia — present in over 30% of patients with visible keloids on the face, neck, or ears.
earlyWarningSigns: Any scar that continues to grow and extend beyond the original wound edge beyond 12 weeks — the defining feature of keloid vs hypertrophic scar and an indication for early specialist treatment.
Significant pruritus developing in a healing wound — may indicate keloid formation beginning; early silicone sheet application may prevent progression.
New nodules appearing within a scar 6–12 months after apparent healing, especially at high-risk sites (sternum, ear, shoulder) — early recurrence warranting reassessment.

Redness and warmth in a scar in a high-risk individual (Afro-Caribbean or Asian background, family history) 2–4 weeks after surgery — early intervention with silicone, massage, and pressure gives the best prevention results.
Rapidly growing, pruritic, warm scar lesion at an acne or vaccination site in a teenager — early treatment prevents cosmetically disfiguring keloid progression.
emergencySigns: Rapid increase in keloid size over days to weeks accompanied by fever, warmth, and purulent discharge — superimposed infection requiring systemic antibiotics before any other intervention.
Ulceration and bleeding from a large keloid — requires wound care management and specialist assessment; rarely may indicate squamous-cell carcinoma arising within a chronic keloid (Marjolin's ulcer).
Severe restriction of neck or axillary range of motion from giant burn keloids — functional impairment requiring urgent surgical planning.
Psychological crisis or suicidal ideation related to keloid-associated body dysmorphia or disfigurement — requires immediate psychiatric assessment alongside dermatological management.

causes.cause: Cutaneous injury — surgical wounds, burns, lacerations
causes.description: Any breach of the dermis in a genetically predisposed individual can initiate keloid formation. Surgical incisions perpendicular to Langer's skin tension lines, closure under tension, and wounds crossing body lines are higher risk. Ear-piercing keloids account for the highest volume of cases referred for treatment.

causes.cause: Inflammatory skin conditions — acne, folliculitis, chickenpox
causes.description: Inflammatory acne pustules and nodules cause dermal disruption that triggers keloid formation on the chest and back in susceptible individuals. Chickenpox scarring is a common childhood cause; herpes zoster scars and BCG vaccination sites can also keloid.

Keloids cluster in families; first-degree relatives of affected individuals have a 3–5× higher risk. Autosomal dominant inheritance with incomplete penetrance has been documented in multiple kindreds. GWAS has identified susceptibility loci on chromosomes 2q23 and 7p11 in Japanese and African populations.

causes.cause: Excessive TGF-β signaling and fibroblast dysregulation
causes.description: Keloid fibroblasts overexpress TGF-β1 and TGF-β2 — the primary drivers of collagen synthesis — and underexpress TGF-β3 (which promotes scar regression). The resulting collagen overproduction from aberrant fibroblasts is the key pathomechanism. Targeted therapy against TGF-β is an active area of investigation.

High mechanical tension across wound edges promotes keloid formation. The sternal and shoulder regions are under continuous motion-related tension; wound closure under tension amplifies fibroblast activation. Pressure therapy works by counteracting these forces mechanically.

riskFactors.factor: Afro-Caribbean, Hispanic, or Asian skin type (Fitzpatrick III–VI)
riskFactors.description: Keloid incidence is 15–20× higher in individuals with darker skin tones; hypothesized to relate to melanocyte-fibroblast paracrine signaling differences. Rates of up to 16% are reported in Black African and Caribbean populations vs 1–2% in Caucasian populations.
A first-degree family member with keloids increases personal risk 3–5×; genetic susceptibility loci identified on chromosomes 2q23 and 7p11 in genome-wide association studies.
Keloid incidence peaks in adolescence and early adulthood when hormonal stimulation of fibroblasts and wound healing is most active; keloids are uncommon in young children and rare after age 60.
The sternal region, deltoid area, upper back, earlobes, and jawline are the highest-risk sites; wounds in these areas should receive prophylactic silicone sheeting in high-risk individuals.
Estrogen and progesterone stimulate dermal fibroblast activity; keloid growth often accelerates during pregnancy and regresses postpartum in some patients.

Earlobe piercing is the most common trigger; cartilage piercing carries even higher keloid risk. In susceptible individuals, any piercing or tattoo should be avoided or performed after careful consideration.
Wound infection, dehiscence, and prolonged inflammation amplify TGF-β-driven fibrosis and substantially increase keloid risk; meticulous aseptic wound care is the most modifiable prevention strategy.

affectedDemographics: Afro-Caribbean and sub-Saharan African populations: 5–16% lifetime keloid prevalence vs 1–2% in Caucasian populations; accounts for the majority of clinical keloid burden globally.
East and Southeast Asian populations: elevated prevalence (estimated 2–8%) with a predilection for earlobe and jawline keloids.
Adolescents and young adults 10–30 years: highest incidence period correlating with hormonal skin changes and high rates of acne, piercing, and surgical procedures.
Women slightly more affected than men in most epidemiological series — likely reflecting higher rates of cosmetic piercing and surgical procedures.
diagnosisOverview: Keloids are diagnosed clinically in most cases. The history documents the inciting wound, time since injury, rate of growth, and symptom profile. The distinguishing feature from hypertrophic scar is extension beyond the original wound boundary — confirmed by examining whether the scar edge exceeds the original wound line or surgical closure. Keloids typically continue growing beyond 12 weeks post-injury; hypertrophic scars plateau and begin to regress. The Vancouver Scar Scale (VSS) or Patient and Observer Scar Assessment Scale (POSAS) standardizes clinical description using vascularity, pigmentation, pliability, height, and surface area. Dermoscopy may reveal the characteristic disorganized collagen pattern. Biopsy is not routinely needed but confirms the diagnosis histologically — disorganized, thick collagen bundles with myofibroblasts and few inflammatory cells — when there is diagnostic uncertainty or the lesion is atypical. Malignant transformation (squamous-cell carcinoma within a chronic keloid — Marjolin's ulcer) is rare but warrants biopsy for any rapidly growing or ulcerated lesion. Ultrasound can measure lesion thickness and vascularity to guide intralesional injection depth and monitor treatment response. Genetic testing for predisposition is not yet clinical standard but is an active research area.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination with Vancouver Scar Scale (VSS) or POSAS scoring
diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardizes documentation of keloid characteristics: vascularity, pliability, height, surface area, and patient symptoms for baseline and treatment monitoring
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines and measures the scar, records color, texture, and height, and asks you to rate symptoms. Takes 10–15 minutes. Repeated at each visit to track treatment response.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reveals disorganized collagen architecture, vascularity pattern, and surface texture to support the clinical diagnosis and exclude other dermatological diagnoses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld magnifying lens with polarized light is held against the scar. Painless and takes under 5 minutes. Helps distinguish keloids from dermatofibromas and other fibrous tumors.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Histological confirmation of keloid diagnosis when clinical presentation is atypical; excludes malignant transformation in ulcerated or rapidly growing lesions
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anesthetic injection, then a small 3–4 mm punch removes a tissue core. Sutured or left to heal. Specimen sent to histopathology; results in 5–7 days. Mild soreness for 2–3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures lesion depth, identifies vascular component, and guides intralesional injection to the optimal depth
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Gel applied to the scar; high-frequency probe produces cross-sectional images. Painless, 5–10 minutes. Particularly useful before cryotherapy or intralesional injection to estimate depth.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents keloid size, color, and morphology at baseline and follow-up for objective treatment response monitoring
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standardized photographs taken by a clinical photographer or clinician using measured lighting and distance. Used to construct the patient's scar-treatment record; important for medicolegal documentation.

treatmentOverview: Keloid treatment is inherently multimodal because no single intervention is sufficiently effective. The 2020 International Advisory Panel on Scar Management (Gold et al.) recommends treatment stratified by lesion size and patient risk. First-line for all keloids is prevention: silicone sheeting applied to healing wounds for 12–24 hours per day for at least 3 months reduces keloid formation in high-risk individuals and is the only treatment with Level 1 evidence for prevention. For established keloids, intralesional corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone acetonide 10–40 mg/mL at 4–6 week intervals for up to 6 cycles) achieves good softening and flattening in 50–70% of small keloids but has a recurrence rate of 50% over 5 years. Cryotherapy — monthly freeze-thaw cycles — produces comparable results and is particularly effective for nodular keloids under 1 cm. For larger keloids, surgical excision followed immediately by adjuvant therapy is the approach with the lowest documented recurrence rates: surgery plus radiotherapy within 24–48 hours achieves recurrence rates of 5–15% in the best series (vs 45–100% for surgery alone). Intralesional 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) and bleomycin injections reduce collagen synthesis with good evidence from RCTs; combination with triamcinolone is superior to either agent alone. Pulsed dye laser (585 or 595 nm) reduces keloid erythema and vascularity, improving pliability and symptoms, but does not produce consistent volume reduction. Fractional CO2 and Er:YAG lasers can improve surface texture and pliability in combination programs. Silicone pressure therapy is first-line for earlobe keloids post-excision and is combined with compression earrings. Biological agents targeting TGF-β and VEGF pathways are in clinical trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone acetonide (10–40 mg/mL, every 4–6 weeks × 4–6 cycles)
medicalTreatments.description: Corticosteroid injected directly into the keloid reduces fibroblast proliferation, collagen synthesis, and mast-cell activity. Softens and flattens the lesion over months. Adverse effects include hypopigmentation (20–30%), skin atrophy, and telangiectasia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate 50–70% for symptom improvement and volume reduction; 5-year recurrence rate 50% as monotherapy (Mustoe et al. PMID 12163395)

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional 5-fluorouracil (50 mg/mL, weekly × 12 sessions)
medicalTreatments.description: Antimetabolite that inhibits fibroblast proliferation by blocking thymidylate synthase. Used alone or combined with triamcinolone. Less effective alone but significantly augments steroid response with fewer steroid adverse effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combination 5-FU + triamcinolone superior to triamcinolone alone: response rate 85% vs 70% in RCT; less hypopigmentation than steroid-only regimen (Bijlard et al. PMID 26219984)

medicalTreatments.name: Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen, 10–30 second freeze-thaw cycles, 4–6 weekly sessions)
medicalTreatments.description: Liquid nitrogen applied via probe or spray destroys keloid fibroblasts through ice-crystal formation and vascular disruption. Intralesional cryotherapy (needle probe inserted into the lesion) produces deeper freeze with fewer cycles than surface spray.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate 60–75% for lesion volume reduction; recurrence rate 25–35% at 12 months; transient hypopigmentation in 70% of patients (Har-Shai et al. 2003, PMID 14709991)

medicalTreatments.name: Silicone sheeting or gel (applied 12–24 h/day for 3–6 months)
medicalTreatments.description: Medical-grade silicone sheet or gel applied continuously to the keloid increases dermal hydration and oxygen tension, suppressing TGF-β activity. First-line prevention for high-risk wounds after surgery or trauma; also used adjunctively with other treatments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Level 1 evidence for prevention: recurrence risk reduced by 50–70% in high-risk wounds when applied consistently from initial healing phase (Gold et al. PMID 26219985)

medicalTreatments.name: Pulsed dye laser (595 nm, 5–7 J/cm², 3–6 sessions at 4–6 week intervals)
medicalTreatments.description: Selectively targets hemoglobin in keloid vasculature; reduces erythema, vascularity, and pruritus. Best evidence for vascular, early-stage keloids and for adjunct symptom control alongside other treatments.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduction in erythema and pruritus in 60–80% of patients; limited evidence for volume reduction alone; best used in combination programs

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional bleomycin (1.5 IU/mL, multiple sessions)
medicalTreatments.description: Cytotoxic antibiotic that induces fibroblast apoptosis through DNA damage. Delivered by multiple puncture technique or needle injection. Evidence supports better volume reduction than triamcinolone monotherapy with an acceptable adverse-effect profile at intralesional doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete flattening in 50–75% and significant improvement in 85% of keloids in prospective series (PMID 10233534); low systemic absorption at intralesional doses

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision with immediate adjuvant radiotherapy (within 24–48 hours)
surgicalOptions.description: Keloid is excised with narrow margins (1–2 mm); low-dose superficial radiotherapy (total 12–15 Gy in 3–5 fractions) is delivered immediately post-excision to ablate residual keloid fibroblasts in the wound bed. The combination is the gold-standard approach for large or recurrent keloids.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence rate 5–15% at 5 years vs 45–100% for excision alone; evidence Grade A from multiple prospective series (Jones et al. PMID 26219984)

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision with intralesional triamcinolone at closure and post-operatively
surgicalOptions.description: Corticosteroid injected into the wound edges at the time of closure and at 4–6 week intervals post-operatively for 6 months. Less effective than radiation for large keloids but appropriate for small lesions or where radiotherapy is not available.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence rate 20–35% at 5 years; better outcomes when combined with silicone sheeting from the day of suture removal

surgicalOptions.name: Intralesional cryotherapy probe (Cryoshape or similar device)
surgicalOptions.description: A hollow needle probe inserted into the keloid core delivers liquid nitrogen through the lesion; more controlled depth of freeze than surface spray. Used for nodular ear and chest keloids as minimally invasive alternative to excision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Volume reduction >75% in 50–60% of patients; recurrence rate 20–30% at 12 months; better results in nodular than flat sheet keloids

alternativeTreatments.name: Onion extract gel (Mederma)
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical quercetin-containing onion extract; multiple small RCTs show modest improvement in scar pliability and pruritus but no evidence of volume reduction compared to plain moisturizer; often used by patients as a first self-initiated treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Botulinum toxin A injection (2.5–5 units/cm² every 3 months)
alternativeTreatments.description: Inhibits fibroblast proliferation through adenylyl cyclase pathway; small RCTs and case series show improved scar pliability and reduced recurrence as adjuvant post-surgical therapy; not yet standard but gaining evidence.

whySeeSpecialist: A plastic surgeon or dermatologist with experience in pathological scar management is needed to select the appropriate multimodal treatment strategy, accurately diagnose keloid vs hypertrophic scar, plan surgical timing in relation to adjuvant therapy, and manage recurrence. A radiation oncologist is required if post-excision radiotherapy is planned. Psychodermatology input benefits patients with significant psychological distress.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a plastic surgeon or dermatologist who manages a high volume of keloid cases and has access to intralesional injection, laser therapy, cryotherapy, and radiotherapy in a coordinated program. For radiation adjuvant therapy, ensure the surgical center has an established referral pathway to a radiation oncologist who can deliver treatment within 24–48 hours of excision — the timing is critical to efficacy.
hospitalCriteria: Plastic surgery or dermatology outpatient clinic with intralesional injection and cryotherapy capability
Access to pulsed dye laser and fractional laser for combination treatment
Radiation oncology department for immediate post-excision superficial radiotherapy
Pathology service for histological confirmation when biopsy is indicated
keyFacilities: Plastic and reconstructive surgery outpatient departments
Dermatology practices with scar management subspecialty
Academic medical center wound and scar clinics
Radiation oncology departments with experience in superficial keloid radiotherapy
Burns rehabilitation centers for post-burn keloid management
preventionStrategies: Apply medical-grade silicone sheeting or gel to any new wound or surgical incision in high-risk individuals (dark skin tone, family history, high-risk anatomical site) from the day of suture removal; continue for 3–6 months.
Use pressure earrings continuously for 6–12 months after earlobe keloid excision to prevent recurrence — without pressure therapy, recurrence rates exceed 80%.
Avoid elective skin piercing and tattooing if you have previously formed a keloid or have an Afro-Caribbean, Hispanic, or Asian skin type with family history of keloids.
Ensure meticulous wound care with antiseptic cleaning, early removal of sutures (5–7 days on face, 10–14 days elsewhere), and protection from UV radiation to minimize keloid stimuli.
Treat active acne aggressively in high-risk individuals to prevent the inflammatory skin breaches that trigger chest and back keloids.
Minimize wound tension by closing surgical incisions along Langer's lines, using subcuticular sutures, and applying tension-relieving tapes (Steri-Strips, paper tape) for 6–12 weeks after closure.
lifestyleModifications: Wear silicone sheeting or pressure garments as prescribed every day — adherence of less than 12 hours per day reduces effectiveness below the treatment threshold.
Protect the keloid from sun exposure with physical sunscreen or clothing to prevent hyperpigmentation within and around the lesion.
Avoid friction and pressure from clothing seams over keloids on the chest and shoulders — padded bra straps or specially designed clothing reduces mechanical irritation.
Control itching with non-sedating antihistamines (loratadine 10 mg daily) rather than scratching; mechanical trauma to an active keloid stimulates further growth.
Maintain regular follow-up appointments (every 3–6 months) with the treating clinician — early detection of early recurrence enables timely re-injection before the keloid re-establishes.
Engage with psychological support or peer groups if keloid-related distress is significant; cognitive-behavioral therapy improves quality of life scores in patients with disfiguring keloids.

recommended: Adequate protein intake (1.0–1.2 g/kg/day) to support wound healing in the perioperative period
Vitamin C (500 mg/day) as a cofactor for collagen hydroxylation during wound remodeling
Zinc-rich foods (meat, shellfish, legumes) to support fibroblast activity and epidermal repair
Anti-inflammatory foods (omega-3 fatty acids, colorful vegetables) to attenuate chronic wound inflammation
avoid: Tobacco and nicotine — reduce tissue oxygenation and impair wound healing, increasing risk of keloid formation post-surgery
Excessive alcohol — impairs collagen synthesis and increases infection risk in the perioperative period
High-sugar and ultra-processed diets — promote systemic inflammation and glycation of skin collagen

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is generally safe and beneficial for overall health. For patients with chest, shoulder, or abdominal keloids, avoid high-resistance upper-body exercise immediately post-excision for 6–8 weeks to minimize wound tension. Swimming in chlorinated pools should be avoided during silicone sheet treatment (silicone must remain dry). After the initial healing phase, return to full activity is encouraged.
dailyManagement: Apply silicone sheeting or gel for at least 12 consecutive hours per day; clean and dry the skin before application.
Attend scheduled intralesional injection or cryotherapy sessions every 4–6 weeks; do not defer appointments as treatment intervals are evidence-based.
Apply pressure garment or compression earring as instructed immediately after excision and for at least 6–12 months post-operatively.
Monitor the treated keloid monthly using photography or a tape-measure record of lesion dimensions to detect recurrence early.
Use antihistamines for pruritus management as needed; avoid scratching and friction.
Disclose all previous keloid history to any surgeon before any future elective procedure so prophylactic adjuvant treatment can be planned.
prognosis: Keloid prognosis depends on lesion size, site, and treatment strategy. Small earlobe keloids excised with post-operative pressure therapy have recurrence rates of 10–20%; chest and shoulder keloids excised with immediate adjuvant radiotherapy recur in 5–15% at 5 years. Without adjuvant therapy, surgical recurrence exceeds 50% at 1 year and 80–100% at 5 years across most series. Intralesional corticosteroid monotherapy produces sustained remission in approximately 50% at 5 years. Factors predicting worse prognosis include dark skin type, positive family history, sternal or shoulder location, spontaneous onset without identifiable injury, and lesions larger than 3 cm. Spontaneous regression without treatment does not occur in established keloids; hypertrophic scars can be mistaken for keloids but do self-improve over 12–18 months, representing a key differential with better natural history.
recoveryTimeline: Intralesional injection: symptomatic improvement (pruritus, softening) within 2–4 weeks; maximum volume reduction at 3–6 months after a course of 4–6 injections. Post-surgical with radiotherapy: wound healing 2–3 weeks; radiotherapy delivered in week 1 post-excision; recurrence surveillance for at least 5 years. Cryotherapy: visible response after 2–3 sessions over 3 months. Silicone prevention: 3–6 months of continuous use for full scar maturation.
complications: Recurrence after treatment — the most common complication; surgery alone produces recurrence in 45–100% of cases without adjuvant therapy; recurrent keloids are often larger than the original.
Hypopigmentation and skin atrophy from repeated intralesional corticosteroid injections — especially in darker skin tones where cosmetic impact is significant; limit to maximum 6 cycles.

Radiation-induced skin changes — dyspigmentation, telangiectasia, and a very small risk of secondary malignancy (estimated 0.01–0.1%) from post-excision radiotherapy; must be balanced against keloid recurrence impact.
Psychological morbidity — depression, social anxiety, and body dysmorphia in over 30% of patients with visible keloids; inadequately addressed in many dermatological and surgical clinics.
Keloid ulceration and infection in large lesions — particularly on the upper back under shirt collar friction; requires antibiotic management and wound care before definitive treatment.
Functional impairment — restriction of movement from large keloids over flexor surfaces (axilla, antecubital fossa, neck) requiring combined surgical release and pressure therapy.

supportResources.name: Keloid Support Group — Changing Faces (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity supporting people with visible difference including keloid scars; psychosocial support, counseling referral, and advocacy resources.

supportResources.name: American Society of Plastic Surgeons — Scar Revision Patient Guide
supportResources.description: ASPS patient information on scar revision options including keloid excision, laser, and injection therapies.

supportResources.name: International Scar Management Advisory Panel — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient and clinician resources from the international panel that produced the 2020 scar management consensus guidelines.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypertrophic Scar
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypertrophic scars remain within the original wound boundary and typically regress spontaneously over 12–18 months; keloids extend beyond the wound edge and grow indefinitely without treatment. Hypertrophic scars can be treated with corticosteroid injection or silicone with high success rates and rarely recur; keloids require multimodal treatment and recur frequently.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acne
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Active acne can trigger keloid formation at inflammatory nodule sites on the chest and back in genetically predisposed individuals. The underlying acne lesion must be treated concurrently to prevent ongoing keloid stimulation; acne without keloid formation does not produce the overgrowing fibrous scar that characterizes a keloid.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic plaques are erythematous, scaly, and pruritic but arise from keratinocyte proliferation without dermal fibrosis; Koebner phenomenon in psoriasis can trigger new plaques at wound sites, mimicking keloid spread. Biopsy differentiates the two: keloids show thick disorganized collagen; psoriatic plaques show epidermal acanthosis and neutrophilic infiltration.

relatedConditions.name: Hypertrophic Scar
relatedConditions.relevance: The principal differential diagnosis; hypertrophic scars share treatment modalities (silicone, steroids) but have a better natural history and lower recurrence after treatment than keloids.

relatedConditions.name: Acne
relatedConditions.relevance: Active nodulocystic acne on the chest and back is a major trigger for keloid formation in susceptible individuals; concurrent acne treatment is essential in keloid management.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Koebner phenomenon in psoriasis can trigger new plaques at wound sites; the differential with keloid is important as treatments differ fundamentally.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Chronic eczematous skin scratching produces repeated dermal microtrauma that can trigger keloid formation in predisposed individuals; concurrent eczema management reduces keloid risk.

faqs.question: What is a keloid scar?
faqs.answer: A keloid is a fibrous scar growth that extends beyond the boundary of the original wound. It results from excess collagen production by genetically predisposed fibroblasts after skin injury. Unlike hypertrophic scars, keloids do not regress spontaneously and may grow over months to years. They are most common on earlobes, chest, shoulders, and upper back.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for keloid scars?
faqs.answer: For small keloids, intralesional triamcinolone injection every 4–6 weeks produces good outcomes. For larger keloids, surgical excision combined with immediate radiotherapy reduces recurrence to 5–15% at 5 years vs 45–100% for surgery alone. Silicone sheeting prevents keloids in high-risk wounds when applied from suture removal for 3–6 months.

faqs.question: How do you prevent keloid scars?
faqs.answer: In high-risk individuals (dark skin tone, family history, high-risk sites), apply medical-grade silicone sheeting for 12+ hours daily for 3–6 months after wound healing. Minimize wound tension, treat acne early, avoid elective piercing, and plan surgical closures along skin tension lines. Pressure earrings after earlobe keloid excision reduce recurrence significantly.

faqs.question: Do keloid injections hurt?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intralesional triamcinolone injection into a keloid is painful because the lesion is dense and fibrous, and the injection must be delivered under high pressure. Topical anesthetic cream (EMLA) applied 60 minutes before the injection reduces discomfort. Most patients tolerate the procedure with a brief sharp pain during injection that resolves within minutes.

faqs.question: Can keloids come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, keloids have a very high surgical recurrence rate — 45–100% for excision alone. Adjuvant therapy started within 24–48 hours of surgery reduces recurrence substantially: surgery plus radiotherapy achieves 5–15% recurrence at 5 years; surgery plus intralesional steroids reduces recurrence to 20–35%. Lifelong monitoring is needed.

faqs.question: What causes keloid scars?
faqs.answer: Keloids result from excess TGF-β-driven collagen production by genetically predisposed fibroblasts after any skin injury — surgery, burns, ear piercing, acne, or vaccination. Genetics are the strongest factor; dark skin type, positive family history, and high-tension anatomical sites (sternum, shoulder) increase risk substantially. Hormones accelerate growth during pregnancy.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a keloid and a hypertrophic scar?
faqs.answer: A keloid extends beyond the original wound boundary and does not regress. A hypertrophic scar stays within the wound edges and typically regresses over 12–18 months. Both are treated with silicone and steroids but hypertrophic scars respond better and rarely recur. Keloids require multimodal treatment and lifelong monitoring.

faqs.question: Why are keloids more common in dark skin?
faqs.answer: Keloids occur 15–20× more frequently in people with Fitzpatrick skin types IV–VI (Afro-Caribbean, Hispanic, South Asian). The mechanism involves paracrine signaling between melanocytes and fibroblasts that upregulates TGF-β and collagen production. Genetic susceptibility loci also differ in frequency across ethnic groups.

faqs.question: Is keloid radiotherapy safe?
faqs.answer: Post-excision superficial radiotherapy for keloid is considered safe when delivered at standard doses (12–15 Gy in 3–5 fractions). The estimated risk of secondary malignancy is approximately 0.01–0.1%, which most clinicians consider acceptable given the high distress and recurrence burden of untreated keloids. It should not be used over the thyroid, gonads, or breast.

faqs.question: How long does keloid treatment take?
faqs.answer: Treatment courses depend on the method. Intralesional injection: 4–6 sessions over 6 months for optimal response. Post-surgical radiotherapy: delivered in 3–5 sessions over 1 week immediately after excision. Silicone sheeting: worn daily for 3–6 months. Recurrence surveillance continues for at least 5 years after any surgical treatment.

faqs.question: Can keloids be treated at home?
faqs.answer: Over-the-counter silicone gel or sheeting (e.g., ScarAway, Mepiform) is the only evidence-supported home treatment; apply for 12+ hours daily for 3–6 months. Onion extract gels have very limited evidence. Intralesional injections, cryotherapy, laser, and surgery require a clinician. Early home silicone use from wound healing reduces the need for clinic intervention.

faqs.question: Does cryotherapy work for keloids?
faqs.answer: Yes. Monthly cryotherapy sessions (liquid nitrogen freeze-thaw cycles) produce volume reduction in 60–75% of keloids, particularly small nodular lesions under 1 cm. Intralesional cryoprobe delivery gives deeper and more uniform freezing than surface spray with fewer sessions. Recurrence rate is 25–35% at 12 months.

faqs.question: Do keloid scars go away on their own?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike hypertrophic scars, established keloids do not regress spontaneously. They may stabilize in size but will not diminish without treatment. This is the clinically important distinguishing feature from hypertrophic scars, which improve over 12–18 months. All keloids require specialist management for meaningful improvement.

faqs.question: What laser is used for keloid scars?
faqs.answer: The pulsed dye laser (595 nm) targets keloid vascularity, reducing redness and pruritus in 60–80% of patients. Fractional CO2 and Er:YAG lasers improve surface texture and pliability. Lasers work best as adjuncts to injection or surgical treatment and are not effective for volume reduction when used alone.

faqs.question: Can I get a keloid from ear piercing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ear-piercing keloids are the most common type referred for treatment. Earlobe keloids typically appear 3–12 months after piercing. Cartilage piercing (helix, tragus) carries even higher keloid risk. Anyone with a personal or family history of keloids should avoid all ear piercing or seek pre-piercing advice from a dermatologist.

metaTitle: Keloid Treatment: Removal, Injections & Recurrence | aihealz
metaDescription: Keloid scars grow beyond the wound edge and cause itching, pain, and disfigurement. Treatment options, recurrence prevention, and specialist guidance reviewed.
